text: The Live High-Train Low (LHTL) altitude training model, where athletes acclimatize to chronic hypoxia while performing high-intensity training at low altitude, has been successfully used by elite endurance athletes for Olympic and World Championship podium performances since 1997.
The classical altitude training approach ('Live High-Train High') originally emerged to prepare athletes for the 1968 Olympics held at 2300 m elevation and focused on improving oxygen transport.
Iron status must be monitored and optimized before and during Live High-Train Low altitude training camps to support the desired hematological adaptations.
Individual responses to Live High-Train Low altitude training vary significantly, meaning not all athletes benefit equally from the same altitude protocol.
Training load and athlete wellness/well-being should be systematically monitored during Live High-Train Low camps to optimize outcomes and prevent maladaptation.
The timing of Live High-Train Low interventions relative to competition is a key factor for successful performance outcomes in elite athletes.
The LHTL model can be implemented using either natural altitude or simulated hypoxia (e.g., altitude tents), though the choice between methods is an important practical consideration.
While Live High-Train Low produces robust physiological adaptations (e.g., hematological changes), translating these adaptations into measurable performance gains is less consistent.
